sections.whoTheyAre: Harbor Aviation is not an organization I can describe with confidence from widely known facts, so I will not guess at its mission or scale. Before the interview, look it up directly: read the About and Services pages on the company website, and check whether it is a fixed-base operator, a regional carrier, an MRO (maintenance, repair, overhaul) shop, or a charter operator. Note its fleet type and roughly how many aircraft or sites it supports, since that shapes the whole role.
sections.whatTheRoleOwns: A Maintenance Planning Lead usually owns the schedule of aircraft maintenance work: sequencing inspections and checks, ordering parts and tooling ahead of need, and coordinating hangar slots and labor so aircraft return to service on time. The role typically balances regulatory compliance intervals against operational demand, and keeps accurate records that survive an audit. Expect daily decisions about what gets deferred, what gets expedited, and how to keep downtime predictable.
sections.whyYourRecordFits: No resume is on file, so treat this as general guidance. If your service included aircraft or equipment maintenance scheduling, parts and supply forecasting, or coordinating crews against inspection intervals, that experience maps closely to this work. Come prepared to name specific systems you tracked, the size of the fleet or inventory you managed, and how you kept work on schedule.
sections.likelyQuestions: Expect: How do you plan maintenance around required inspection intervals while meeting operational deadlines? A strong answer names a real interval-driven schedule you built and the outcome. How do you handle a parts shortage or an unplanned grounding? Answer with a specific instance and what you actually did. How do you keep maintenance records audit-ready? Cite the recordkeeping system and standard you worked under, not just that you were careful.
sections.honestGap: The likely gap is civilian aviation regulatory fluency, especially FAA maintenance rules and documentation, if your experience is entirely military. State it plainly: say your planning and compliance discipline transfers, that you are learning the specific FAA framework, and give one concrete example of how quickly you absorbed a new regulatory standard before.
